**Xi'an**[[a]](#cite_note-4) is the [capital](/wiki/List_of_capitals_in_China "List of capitals in China") of the Chinese province of [Shaanxi](/wiki/Shaanxi "Shaanxi"). A [sub-provincial city](/wiki/Sub-provincial_city "Sub-provincial city") on the [Guanzhong](/wiki/Guanzhong "Guanzhong") plain,[[4]](#cite_note-5) the city is the third-most populous city in [Western China](/wiki/Western_China "Western China") after [Chongqing](/wiki/Chongqing "Chongqing") and [Chengdu](/wiki/Chengdu "Chengdu"), as well as the most populous city in [Northwestern China](/wiki/Northwestern_China "Northwestern China").[[5]](#cite_note-6) Its total population was 12.95 million in the 2020 census, including an urban population of 9.28 million.[[6]](#cite_note-7)

Xi'an is one of the oldest cities in China.[[7]](#cite_note-8) Known as [Chang'an](/wiki/Chang%27an "Chang'an") throughout much of its history, Xi'an is one of China's [Four Great Ancient Capitals](/wiki/Historical_capitals_of_China "Historical capitals of China"), having held the position under several of the most important [dynasties in Chinese history](/wiki/Dynasties_in_Chinese_history "Dynasties in Chinese history"),[[8]](#cite_note-encarta-9) including the [Western Zhou](/wiki/Western_Zhou "Western Zhou"), [Qin](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty"), [Western Han](/wiki/Western_Han "Western Han"), [Sui](/wiki/Sui_dynasty "Sui dynasty"), [Northern Zhou](/wiki/Northern_Zhou "Northern Zhou") and [Tang](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty").[[8]](#cite_note-encarta-9) Xi'an is now the second-most popular tourist destination in China.[[9]](#cite_note-10) The city was one of the terminal points on the [Silk Road](/wiki/Silk_Road "Silk Road") during the ancient and medieval eras, as well as the home of the 3rd-century BC [Terracotta Army](/wiki/Terracotta_Army "Terracotta Army") commissioned by Emperor [Qin Shi Huang](/wiki/Qin_Shi_Huang "Qin Shi Huang")—both of which are listed as [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO").[[10]](#cite_note-br-11)

Since the 1980s, as part of the [economic growth of inland China](/wiki/China_Western_Development "China Western Development") especially for the central and northwest regions, Xi'an has developed into a cultural, industrial, political and educational, and [research and development](/wiki/Research_and_development "Research and development") hub. Xi'an currently holds [sub-provincial](/wiki/Sub-provincial_city_in_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Sub-provincial city in the People's Republic of China") status, administering 11 districts and 2 counties.[[11]](#cite_note-12) In 2020, Xi'an was ranked as a Beta- (global second tier) city by the [Globalization and World Cities Research Network](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network "Globalization and World Cities Research Network"),[[12]](#cite_note-:1-13) and, according to the country's own ranking, ranked 17th;[[13]](#cite_note-14) it is also one of the world's top 100 financial centers according to the [Global Financial Centres Index](/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index").[[14]](#cite_note-15) Xi'an is ranked as the 16th [city globally by scientific output](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_scientific_output "List of cities by scientific output") and first in Western China,[[15]](#cite_note-16) and it is home to multiple [prestigious educational institutions](/wiki/List_of_universities_and_colleges_in_Shaanxi "List of universities and colleges in Shaanxi"), such as [Xi'an Jiaotong University](/wiki/Xi%27an_Jiaotong_University "Xi'an Jiaotong University"), [Northwestern Polytechnical University](/wiki/Northwestern_Polytechnical_University "Northwestern Polytechnical University"), [Xidian University](/wiki/Xidian_University "Xidian University") and [Northwest University](/wiki/Northwest_University_(China) "Northwest University (China)").[[16]](#cite_note-:2-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18)

## Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Name")]

*Xi'an* is the [atonal](/wiki/Standard_Chinese_phonology "Standard Chinese phonology") [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin") [romanization](/wiki/Romanization_of_Chinese "Romanization of Chinese") of its Chinese name [西安](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%A5%BF%E5%AE%89#Chinese "wikt:西安"), *Western Peace*. (The apostrophe – known in Chinese as a [隔音符號](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%9A%94%E9%9F%B3%E7%AC%A6%E8%99%9F#Traditional_Chinese "wikt:隔音符號"), *géyīn fúhào* – should be included to distinguish its pronunciation from the single syllable *xian*.) The name was adopted in 1369 under the early [Ming dynasty](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty"). [Jesuit missionaries](/wiki/Jesuit_China_missions "Jesuit China missions") recorded its name as *Si-ngan* or *Si-ngan-fou*[[18]](#cite_note-19) from its status as the seat of a [prefecture](/wiki/Fu_(administrative_division) "Fu (administrative division)") ([府](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%BA%9C#Chinese "wikt:府"), *fǔ*). This form still appears in the Latin name of the [Catholic diocese of Xi'an](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Xi%27an "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Xi'an"), *archidioecesis Singanensis*. The name was later romanized as *Hsi-an* by [Wade & Giles](/wiki/Wade-Giles "Wade-Giles") and as *Sianfu*[[19]](#cite_note-20) or *Sian*[[10]](#cite_note-br-11) by the [imperial post office](/wiki/Chinese_postal_romanization "Chinese postal romanization"), both of which were common until the promulgation of pinyin.

The area of present-day Xi'an has been the site of several important former Chinese cities. The capital of the [Western Zhou](/wiki/Western_Zhou_dynasty "Western Zhou dynasty") were the [twin cities](/wiki/Twin_cities "Twin cities") of [Feng](/wiki/Fengjing_(Zhou) "Fengjing (Zhou)") and [Hao](/wiki/Haojing "Haojing"), known collectively as [Fenghao](/wiki/Fenghao "Fenghao"), located on opposite banks of the Feng River at its confluence with the southern bank of the [Wei](/wiki/Wei_River "Wei River") in the western suburbs of present-day Xi'an.[[20]](#cite_note-21) The [Qin](/wiki/State_of_Qin "State of Qin") capital [Xianyang](/wiki/Xianyang "Xianyang") was erected north of the Wei during the [Warring States period](/wiki/Warring_States_period "Warring States period") and was succeeded by the [Western Han](/wiki/Western_Han "Western Han") capital of [Chang'an](/wiki/Chang%27an "Chang'an") ([長安](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%95%B7%E5%AE%89#Traditional_Chinese "wikt:長安")), meaning "Perpetual Peace", which was located south of the Wei and covered the central area of present-day Xi'an. During the [Eastern Han](/wiki/Eastern_Han "Eastern Han"), Chang'an was also known as the "Western Capital" ([西](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%A5%BF#Chinese "wikt:西")[京](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%BA%AC#Chinese "wikt:京")), named for its namesake position relative to the main capital at [Luoyang](/wiki/Luoyang "Luoyang"). Under the [Sui](/wiki/Sui_dynasty "Sui dynasty"), its name became *Daxing* ([大](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A4%A7#Traditional_Chinese "wikt:大")[興](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%88%88#Traditional_Chinese "wikt:興"), "Greatly Prosperous") in AD 581. Under the [Tang](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty"), the name reverted to Chang'an in 618.[[10]](#cite_note-br-11) Under the [Mongolian](/wiki/Mongol_Empire "Mongol Empire") [Yuan dynasty](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty") (13th & 14th centuries), it held a succession of names: *Fengyuan* ([奉](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A5%89#Chinese "wikt:奉")[元](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%85%83#Chinese "wikt:元")), *Anxi* ([安西](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%AE%89%E8%A5%BF#Chinese "wikt:安西"), "Peaceful West") and [Jingzhao](/wiki/Jingzhao "Jingzhao") ([京](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%BA%AC#Chinese "wikt:京")[兆](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%85%86#Chinese "wikt:兆")). The Ming name "Xi'an" was changed back to Xijing ("Western Capital", as above) between 1930 and 1943.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main articles: [History of Xi'an](/wiki/History_of_Xi%27an "History of Xi'an") and [Chang'an](/wiki/Chang%27an "Chang'an")

### Prehistory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Prehistory")]

The [Lantian Man](/wiki/Lantian_Man "Lantian Man") was discovered in 1963 in [Lantian County](/wiki/Lantian_County "Lantian County"), 50 km (31 mi) southeast of Xi'an, and dates back to at least 500,000 years before the present time. A 6,500-year-old [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") village, [Banpo](/wiki/Banpo "Banpo"), was discovered in 1953 on the eastern outskirts of the city proper, which contains the remains of several well organized [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") settlements [carbon dated](/wiki/Radiocarbon_dating "Radiocarbon dating") to 5,600–6,700 [years ago](/wiki/Before_Present "Before Present").[[21]](#cite_note-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-25) The site is now home to the [Xi'an Banpo Museum](/wiki/Xi%27an_Banpo_Museum "Xi'an Banpo Museum"), built in 1957 to preserve the archaeological collection.[[25]](#cite_note-26)

### Ancient era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ancient era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Western_Zhou_dynasty_Carriages_pit2_Xi%27an.JPG/250px-Western_Zhou_dynasty_Carriages_pit2_Xi%27an.JPG)](/wiki/File:Western_Zhou_dynasty_Carriages_pit2_Xi%27an.JPG)

Remains of carriages and horses in [Fenghao](/wiki/Fenghao "Fenghao") during the [Western Zhou](/wiki/Western_Zhou "Western Zhou") (11th–8th cent. BC)

Xi'an became a cultural and political center of China in the 11th century BC with the founding of the [Zhou dynasty](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty"). The capital of Zhou was established in the twin settlements of Fengjing (灃京) and [Haojing](/wiki/Haojing "Haojing") (鎬京), together known as [Fenghao](/wiki/Fenghao "Fenghao"), located southwest of contemporary Xi'an. The settlement was also known as Zōngzhōu (宗周) to indicate its role as the capital of the [vassal states](/wiki/Ancient_Chinese_states "Ancient Chinese states").[[26]](#cite_note-27) In 738 BC, [King Ping of Zhou](/wiki/King_Ping_of_Zhou "King Ping of Zhou") moved the capital to [Luoyang](/wiki/Luoyang "Luoyang") due to [political unrest](/wiki/King_Xie_of_Zhou "King Xie of Zhou").[[27]](#cite_note-28)[[28]](#cite_note-29)

### Imperial era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Imperial era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/History_of_Xi%27an.jpg/250px-History_of_Xi%27an.jpg)](/wiki/File:History_of_Xi%27an.jpg)

A map of the [city walls](/wiki/Chinese_city_walls "Chinese city walls") of settlements in Xi'an from the [Zhou](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty") to [Qing](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") [dynasties](/wiki/List_of_Chinese_dynasties "List of Chinese dynasties")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Xian_guerreros_terracota_general.JPG/250px-Xian_guerreros_terracota_general.JPG)](/wiki/File:Xian_guerreros_terracota_general.JPG)

[Terracotta Army](/wiki/Terracotta_Army "Terracotta Army") inside the [Qin Shi Huang](/wiki/Qin_Shi_Huang "Qin Shi Huang") Mausoleum, third century BC

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Map_of_Chang%27an_in_Tang_Dynasty.svg/250px-Map_of_Chang%27an_in_Tang_Dynasty.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Chang%27an_in_Tang_Dynasty.svg)

Map of [Chang'an](/wiki/Chang%27an "Chang'an") under the [Tang](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") (7th–10th cent.)

Following the [Warring States period](/wiki/Warring_States_period "Warring States period"), China was unified under the [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty") (221–206 BC) for the first time, with the capital located at [Xianyang](/wiki/Xianyang "Xianyang"), just northwest of modern Xi'an.[[29]](#cite_note-diming-30) The first emperor of China, [Qin Shi Huang](/wiki/Qin_Shi_Huang "Qin Shi Huang") ordered the construction of the [Terracotta Army](/wiki/Terracotta_Army "Terracotta Army") and [his mausoleum](/wiki/Mausoleum_of_the_First_Qin_Emperor "Mausoleum of the First Qin Emperor") just to the east of Xi'an almost immediately after his ascension to the throne.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

In 202 BC, the founding emperor [Liu Bang](/wiki/Emperor_Gaozu_of_Han "Emperor Gaozu of Han") of the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") established his capital in Chang'an County; his first palace, Changle Palace (長樂宮, "Perpetual Happiness") was built across the river from the ruin of the Qin capital. This is traditionally regarded as the founding date of Chang'an. Two years later, Liu Bang built [Weiyang Palace](/wiki/Weiyang_Palace "Weiyang Palace") (未央宮, "Never Ending Palace") north of modern Xi'an. Weiyang Palace was the largest palace ever built on Earth, covering 4.8 square kilometers (1,200 acres), which is 6.7 times the size of the current [Forbidden City](/wiki/Forbidden_City "Forbidden City") and 11 times the size of the Vatican City.[[31]](#cite_note-32) The original Xi'an city wall was started in 194 BC and took 4 years to finish. Upon completion, the wall measured 25.7 km (15.97 mi) in length and 12 to 16 m (39.37–52.49 ft) in thickness at the base, enclosing an area of 36 km2 (13.90 sq mi). In the year 190, amidst uprisings and rebellions just prior to the [Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Three_Kingdoms "Three Kingdoms") period, [Dong Zhuo](/wiki/Dong_Zhuo "Dong Zhuo"), a powerful warlord from nearby Xiliang, moved the court from [Luoyang](/wiki/Luoyang "Luoyang") to Chang'an in a bid to avoid [a coalition of other powerful warlords against him](/wiki/Campaign_against_Dong_Zhuo "Campaign against Dong Zhuo").

In 582, shortly after the [Sui dynasty](/wiki/Sui_dynasty "Sui dynasty") was founded, the emperor of Sui ordered a new capital to be built southeast of the Han capital, called Daxing (大興, "Great prosperity"). It consisted of three sections: the Imperial City, the palace section, and the civilian section, with a total area of 84 km2 (32 sq mi) within the city walls. At the time, it was the largest city in the world. The city was renamed Chang'an by the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty").[[32]](#cite_note-33) In the mid-7th century, after returning from his pilgrimage to India, the [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") monk [Xuanzang](/wiki/Xuanzang "Xuanzang") established a translation school for [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") scriptures.

Construction of the [Giant Wild Goose Pagoda](/wiki/Giant_Wild_Goose_Pagoda "Giant Wild Goose Pagoda") began in 652. This [pagoda](/wiki/Pagoda "Pagoda") was 64 m (209.97 ft) in height, and was built to store the translations of Buddhist sutras obtained from India by [Xuanzang](/wiki/Xuanzang "Xuanzang"). In 707, construction of the [Small Wild Goose Pagoda](/wiki/Small_Wild_Goose_Pagoda "Small Wild Goose Pagoda") began. This pagoda measured 45 m (147.64 ft) tall at the time of completion, and was built to store the translations of Buddhist sutras by [Yijing](/wiki/Yijing_(monk) "Yijing (monk)"). The massive [1556 Shaanxi earthquake](/wiki/1556_Shaanxi_earthquake "1556 Shaanxi earthquake") eventually damaged the tower and reduced its height to 43.4 m (142.39 ft).[[33]](#cite_note-kiang_12-34)

The [Nestorian Stele](/wiki/Nestorian_Stele "Nestorian Stele") is a [Tang Chinese](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") [stele](/wiki/Stele "Stele") erected in 781 that documents 150 years of early [Christianity in China](/wiki/Christianity_in_China "Christianity in China").[[34]](#cite_note-hill-108-35) It is a 279 cm tall [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") block with text in both [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") and [Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_language "Syriac language") describing the existence of Christian communities in several cities in northern China. It reveals that the initial [Nestorian Christian](/wiki/Nestorianism "Nestorianism") church had met recognition by the Tang [Emperor Taizong](/wiki/Emperor_Taizong_of_Tang "Emperor Taizong of Tang"), due to efforts of the Christian missionary [Alopen](/wiki/Alopen "Alopen") in 635.[[35]](#cite_note-lhc-36)

Chang'an was devastated at the [end of the Tang dynasty](/wiki/Fall_of_Tang "Fall of Tang"). In the first years of the 10th century, residents were forced to move to the new capital city in [Luoyang](/wiki/Luoyang "Luoyang"). Only a small area of the former city continued to be occupied thereafter.

Under the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty"), Xi'an was an important [cultural center](/wiki/Cultural_center "Cultural center") of scholarship and innovation on matters such as [science](/wiki/Science "Science"),[[36]](#cite_note-:4-37) as well as [historiography](/wiki/Historiography "Historiography"), religion,[[37]](#cite_note-38) and [philosophy](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy") in China. The [Northern Song](/wiki/Song_dynasty#Northern_Song,_960–1127 "Song dynasty") era saw its people, [political culture](/wiki/Political_culture "Political culture"), and strategic location be directly utilized by the Song dynasty proper[[36]](#cite_note-:4-37) and its continued relevance to [Muslim](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims") travelers into China and [Chinese Muslim](/wiki/Chinese_Muslim "Chinese Muslim") residents.[[38]](#cite_note-:02-39)

During the [Ming dynasty](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty"), a new wall was constructed in 1370 which remains intact to this day. The wall measures 11.9 km (7.4 mi) in [circumference](/wiki/Circumference "Circumference"), 12 m (39.37 ft) in height, and 15 to 18 m (49.21–59.06 ft) in thickness at the base; a moat was also built outside the walls. The new wall and moat would protect a much smaller city of 12 km2 (4.6 sq mi).

### Modern era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Modern era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E_%E6%98%8E%E7%A7%A6%E7%8E%8B%E5%BA%9C%E5%9F%8E%E5%A2%99%E9%81%97%E5%9D%80_02.jpg/250px-%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E_%E6%98%8E%E7%A7%A6%E7%8E%8B%E5%BA%9C%E5%9F%8E%E5%A2%99%E9%81%97%E5%9D%80_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E_%E6%98%8E%E7%A7%A6%E7%8E%8B%E5%BA%9C%E5%9F%8E%E5%A2%99%E9%81%97%E5%9D%80_02.jpg)

Site of Mansion of [Prince of Qin](/wiki/Prince_of_Qin_(Ming_dynasty) "Prince of Qin (Ming dynasty)") in Ming dynasty, it was used as Manchu [banner](/wiki/Eight_Banners "Eight Banners") garrison during [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty")

The [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") established a walled off Manchu banner quarter in northeast Xi'an, on the site of the former palace of the Ming Prince of Qin. A Han banner quarter was established in the southeast of the city.

Manchu bannermen from the Xi'an banner garrison were praised for maintaining Manchu culture by [Kangxi](/wiki/Kangxi "Kangxi") in 1703.[[39]](#cite_note-40) Xi'an garrison Manchus were said to retain Manchu culture far better than all other Manchus at martial skills in the provincial garrisons and they were able to draw their bows properly and perform cavalry archery, unlike Beijing Manchus. The Qianlong emperor received a memorial staying Xi'an Manchu bannermen still had martial skills, although not up to those, in the past in a 1737 memorial from Cimbu.[[40]](#cite_note-41) By the 1780s, the military skills of Xi'an Manchu bannermen dropped enormously, and they had been regarded as the most militarily skilled provincial Manchu banner garrison.[[41]](#cite_note-42) Manchu women from the Xi'an garrison often left the walled Manchu garrison and went to [hot springs](/wiki/Huaqing_Pool "Huaqing Pool") outside the city, and gained bad reputations for their sexual lives. A Manchu from Beijing, Sumurji, was shocked and disgusted by this after being appointed Lieutenant general of the Manchu garrison of Xi'an and informed the Yongzheng emperor what they were doing.[[42]](#cite_note-43)[[43]](#cite_note-44) Han civilians and Manchu bannermen in Xi'an had bad relations, with the bannermen trying to steal at the markets. Manchu Lieutenant general Cimbru reported this to Yongzheng emperor in 1729, after he was assigned there. Governor Yue Rui of Shandong was then ordered by the [Yongzheng](/wiki/Yongzheng_Emperor "Yongzheng Emperor") to report any bannerman misbehaving and warned him not to cover it up in 1730, after Manchu bannermen were put in a quarter in Qingzhou.[[44]](#cite_note-45) Manchu bannermen from the garrisons in Xi'an and Jingzhou fought in Xinjiang in the 1770s and Manchus from Xi'an garrison fought in other campaigns against the [Dzungars](/wiki/Dzungar_people "Dzungar people") and [Uyghurs](/wiki/Uyghurs "Uyghurs") throughout the 1690s and 18th century. In the 1720s Jingzhou, Hangzhou, and Nanjing Manchu banner garrisons fought in Tibet.[[45]](#cite_note-46)

For the over 200 years they lived next to each other, Han civilians and Manchu bannermen of both genders in Xi'an did not intermarry with each other at all. The Qing dynasty altered its law on intermarriage between Han civilians and Manchu bannermen several times in the dynasty. At the beginning of the Qing dynasty, the Qing allowed Han civilians to marry Manchu women. Then the Qing banned civilians from marrying women from the Eight banners later. In 1865, the Qing allowed Han civilian men to marry Manchu bannerwomen in all garrisons, except the capital garrison of Beijing. There was no formal law on marriage between people in the different banners, like the Manchu and Han banners, but it was informally regulated by social status and custom. In northeastern China, such as Heilongjiang and Liaoning, it was more common for Manchu women to marry Han men, since they were not subjected to the same laws and institutional oversight as Manchus and Han in Beijing and elsewhere.[[46]](#cite_note-47)

In October 1911, during the [Xinhai revolution](/wiki/Xinhai_revolution "Xinhai revolution"), revolutionaries stormed the Manchu fort in Xi'an. Most of the city's 20,000 Manchus were killed.[[47]](#cite_note-Ernest_Frank_Borst-Smith_1912-48)[[48]](#cite_note-49) Hui (Muslims; then referred to as "Mohammedans") were divided in its support for the revolution. Those of Shaanxi supported the revolutionaries, while those of Gansu supported the Qing. The Hui of Xi'an (Shaanxi province) joined the Han Chinese revolutionaries in slaughtering the Manchus.[[49]](#cite_note-50)[[50]](#cite_note-51)[[51]](#cite_note-52) Some wealthy Manchus survived by being [ransomed](/wiki/Ransom "Ransom"). Wealthy Han Chinese enslaved Manchu girls[[52]](#cite_note-53) and poor Han Chinese troops seized young Manchu women as wives.[[53]](#cite_note-54) Hui Muslims also seized young pretty Manchu girls and raised them as Muslims.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

A British missionary who witnessed the massacre commented that "Old and young, men and women, children alike, were all butchered... Houses were plundered and then burnt; those who would fain have laid hidden till the storm was past, were forced to come out into the open. The revolutionaries, protected by a parapet of the wall, poured a heavy, unceasing, relentless fire into the doomed Tartar (Manchu) city, those who tried to escape thence into the Chinese city were cut down as they emerged from the gates."[[47]](#cite_note-Ernest_Frank_Borst-Smith_1912-48)[[55]](#cite_note-56)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Zhang_Xueliang_and_Yang_Hucheng.jpg/250px-Zhang_Xueliang_and_Yang_Hucheng.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zhang_Xueliang_and_Yang_Hucheng.jpg)

[Zhang Xueliang](/wiki/Zhang_Xueliang "Zhang Xueliang") and [Yang Hucheng](/wiki/Yang_Hucheng "Yang Hucheng") led [Xi'an Incident](/wiki/Xi%27an_Incident "Xi'an Incident") in 1936, which played an vital role in modern history of China

In 1936, the [Xi'an (then "Sian") Incident](/wiki/Xi%27an_Incident "Xi'an Incident") took place in the city during the [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War"). The incident helped to bring the [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang") (KMT) and [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") to form the [Second United Front](/wiki/Second_United_Front "Second United Front") in order to concentrate on fighting against the [Imperial Japanese Army](/wiki/Imperial_Japanese_Army "Imperial Japanese Army") in the [Second Sino-Japanese War](/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War").[[56]](#cite_note-57)

On March 11, 1938, an aerial battle broke out for the first time over Xi'an as [Imperial Japanese Army Air Force](/wiki/Imperial_Japanese_Army_Air_Service "Imperial Japanese Army Air Service") aircraft attacked the city, and was engaged by [Chinese Air Force](/wiki/Development_of_Chinese_Nationalist_air_force_(1937%E2%80%931945) "Development of Chinese Nationalist air force (1937–1945)") [I-15](/wiki/Polikarpov_I-15 "Polikarpov I-15") fighter planes, led by Lt. [Cen Zeliu](/wiki/Cen_Zeliu "Cen Zeliu") of the 5th Pursuit Group, 17th Squadron.[[57]](#cite_note-58) While repeatedly attacked by air, Shaanxi was heavily fortified by units of the [Eighth Route Army](/wiki/Eighth_Route_Army "Eighth Route Army"); Xi'an was never taken by the Japanese forces.[[58]](#cite_note-59)

On May 20, 1949, the Communist-controlled [People's Liberation Army](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army "People's Liberation Army") captured the city of Xi'an from the [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang") force.[[59]](#cite_note-60)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/51514-Xian-Pano_%2827759087230%29.jpg/250px-51514-Xian-Pano_%2827759087230%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:51514-Xian-Pano_(27759087230).jpg)

A panorama of modern Xi'an

During the Mao era, Xi'an was further developed as part of the [Third Front Construction](/wiki/Third_Front_(China) "Third Front (China)").[[60]](#cite_note-:9-61): 186

Xi'an made headlines for being one of the many cities where the [2012 China anti-Japanese demonstrations](/wiki/2012_China_anti-Japanese_demonstrations "2012 China anti-Japanese demonstrations") occurred.[[61]](#cite_note-sinapatriot-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64)

In 2022, Xi'an witnessed the largest [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19") community outbreak since the initial months of the pandemic hit China.[[64]](#cite_note-65) From December 23, 2021, the city was put into strict lockdown after local authorities reported more than 250 cases,[[65]](#cite_note-66) traced to the [Delta variant](/wiki/Delta_variant "Delta variant") by authorities.[[66]](#cite_note-cnbc_20211222-67) This led to stressed healthcare and delayed or insufficient food deliveries to some part of the city.[[67]](#cite_note-68) Restrictions of Xi'an were lifted on January 24.[[68]](#cite_note-69)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Meibei_Lake%2CHuyi_District%2CXi%27an.jpg/250px-Meibei_Lake%2CHuyi_District%2CXi%27an.jpg)](/wiki/File:Meibei_Lake,Huyi_District,Xi%27an.jpg)

Meibei Lake, [Huyi District](/wiki/Huyi_District "Huyi District"), Xi'an

Xi'an lies on the [Guanzhong Plain](/wiki/Guanzhong "Guanzhong") in the south-central part of [Shaanxi](/wiki/Shaanxi "Shaanxi") province, on a flood plain created by the eight surrounding rivers and streams.

The city borders the northern foot of the [Qin Mountains](/wiki/Qin_Mountains "Qin Mountains") (Qinling) to the south, and the banks of the [Wei River](/wiki/Wei_River "Wei River") to the north. [Hua Shan](/wiki/Mount_Hua "Mount Hua"), one of the five sacred Taoist mountains, is located 100 km (62 mi) away to the east of the city. Not far to the north is the [Loess Plateau](/wiki/Loess_Plateau "Loess Plateau").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Txu-oclc-10552568-ni49-5.jpg/250px-Txu-oclc-10552568-ni49-5.jpg)](/wiki/File:Txu-oclc-10552568-ni49-5.jpg)

Map including Xi'an (labeled HSI-AN (SIAN) (walled)) ([AMS](/wiki/Army_Map_Service "Army Map Service"), 1955)

At the beginning of Han dynasty, the Chief of Staff [Zhang Liang](/wiki/Zhang_Liang_(Western_Han) "Zhang Liang (Western Han)") advised the emperor [Liu Bang](/wiki/Liu_Bang "Liu Bang") to choose Guanzhong as the capital of the Han dynasty: "Guanzhong Plain is located behind [Mount Xiao](/wiki/Mount_Xiao "Mount Xiao") and [Hangu Pass](/wiki/Hangu_Pass "Hangu Pass"), and connects Long ([Gansu](/wiki/Gansu "Gansu")) and Shu ([Sichuan](/wiki/Sichuan "Sichuan")). The area can be called an iron castle spreads for thousands of miles, and is rich in harvest like the nation of heaven." (关中左崤函，右陇蜀，沃野千里，此所谓金城千里，天府之国也) Since then, Guanzhong is also known as the 'Nation of the Heaven'.[[69]](#cite_note-70)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Climate")]

Xi'an has a [temperate](/wiki/Temperateness "Temperateness") climate that is influenced by the [East Asian monsoon](/wiki/East_Asian_monsoon "East Asian monsoon"), classified under the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") as a borderline [humid subtropical](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") and [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") (*Cwa/Dwa*). The Wei River valley is characterized by hot, [humid](/wiki/Humidity "Humidity") summers, cold, dry winters, and dry springs and autumns. Most of the annual [precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation_(meteorology) "Precipitation (meteorology)") is delivered from July to late October. Snow occasionally falls in winter but rarely settles for long. [Dust storms](/wiki/Dust_storm "Dust storm") often occur during March and April as the city rapidly warms up. Summer months also experience frequent but short [thunderstorms](/wiki/Thunderstorm "Thunderstorm"). The monthly 24-hour average temperature ranges from around the freezing mark in January to 27.0 °C (80.6 °F) in July, with an annual mean of 14.08 °C (57.3 °F). Extremes since 1951 have ranged from −20.6 °C (−5 °F) on January 11, 1955, to 42.3 °C (108 °F) at the current station in Jinghe on July 15, 2025.[[70]](#cite_note-71) A highest record of 42.9 °C (109 °F) was registered in another station on June 17, 2006.[[71]](#cite_note-72)[[72]](#cite_note-Mherrera-73) An unofficial record low of −25.0 °C (−13 °F) was also recorded in January 1930, but at another weather station in the northern suburbs of the city.[[73]](#cite_note-74)

### National Time Service Center

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: National Time Service Center")]

The [Shaanxi Astronomical Observatory](/wiki/National_Time_Service_Center "National Time Service Center") was established in 1966. In 1975, according to the Geodetic Origin Report of the People's Republic of China, 'in order to avoid bias in the mensuration as much as possible, the Geodetic Origin would be in central mainland China.' Lintong (临潼), a town near Xi'an was chosen. Since 1986, Chinese Standard Time (CST) was set from NTSC. The National Time Service Center (NTSC), the Chinese Academy of Sciences is an institute which is mainly engaged in the service and research on time and frequency. NTSC takes charge of generating and maintaining the national standard time scale, disseminating the time and frequency signals. The autonomous standard time scales of universal time and atomic time and the dissemination techniques with LF radio and HF radio were established successively during the 1970s and 1980s, which meet all the requirements for different applications on the whole, such as the scientific researches, national economy, etc.[[77]](#cite_note-78)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Demographics")]

According to the [Seventh National Census](/wiki/2020_Chinese_census "2020 Chinese census") in 2020, the city's Permanent Population ([hukou](/wiki/Hukou "Hukou")) was 12,952,907. The proportion of males is 51.07% and that of females is 48.93%. In terms of age structure, the proportion of 0–14 years old is 15.65%, that of 15–59 years old is 68.33%, that of over 60 years old is 16.02%, and that of over 65 years old is 10.9%.[[78]](#cite_note-79)

The Xi'an [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area") was estimated by the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") (Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development) to have, as of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit), a population of 12.9 million,[[79]](#cite_note-oecd2015-80) of which 5,740,000 is urban.[[80]](#cite_note-81)

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Xi'an has many areas that are easily accessible on foot. In many commercial, residential, educational zones in the city, especially in the shopping and entertainment districts around the Bell Tower, underpasses and overpasses have been built for the safety and convenience of pedestrians.

A [bicycle sharing](/wiki/Bicycle-sharing_system "Bicycle-sharing system") network started operating in Xi'an from the year 2013 and today has 52,000 bikes, used by over 200,000 people per day.[[86]](#cite_note-:0-87) Taxi services are numerous, but many citizens of Xi'an still commute to work using the city's 270 official municipal bus routes. The routes are serviced by a fleet of over 7,800 buses, with an average system-wide ridership of over 4 million people per day.[[86]](#cite_note-:0-87) The bus network is complemented by a rapidly expanding subway system that carries over 1.5 million commuters per day.[[86]](#cite_note-:0-87) There are more than 2 million registered automobiles[[87]](#cite_note-88) in Xi'an; the growing number of personal automobiles also means traffic jams are a common urban issue.

### Metro

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Metro")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Train_of_Line_14_in_Xi%27an_Metro%2C_May_3_2023_%28New_JC%29.jpg/250px-Train_of_Line_14_in_Xi%27an_Metro%2C_May_3_2023_%28New_JC%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Train_of_Line_14_in_Xi%27an_Metro,_May_3_2023_(New_JC).jpg)

Xi'an Metro [Line 14](/wiki/Line_14_(Xi%27an_Metro) "Line 14 (Xi'an Metro)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Platform_of_FENGHUANGCHI_Station%2C_Xi%27an_Metro_%28Jul_25%2C_2023%29.jpg/250px-Platform_of_FENGHUANGCHI_Station%2C_Xi%27an_Metro_%28Jul_25%2C_2023%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Platform_of_FENGHUANGCHI_Station,_Xi%27an_Metro_(Jul_25,_2023).jpg)

Fenghuangchi station

Main article: [Xi'an Metro](/wiki/Xi%27an_Metro "Xi'an Metro")

Line 2, running through the city from north (North Railway Station) to south (Weiqu Nan), was the first line opened to the public on September 16, 2011.[[88]](#cite_note-cnr-89) Operations began on September 28, 2011.[[88]](#cite_note-cnr-89) This line is 19.9 kilometers (12.4 miles) long with 17 stations.[[89]](#cite_note-90) Line 1 opened on September 15, 2013. As a west–east railway, its 19 stations connect [Houweizhai](/wiki/Houweizhai_Station "Houweizhai Station") and [Fangzhicheng](/wiki/Fangzhicheng_Station "Fangzhicheng Station"). Line 3 runs from northeast ([Baoshuiqu](/wiki/Baoshuiqu_Station_(Xi%27an) "Baoshuiqu Station (Xi'an)")) to southwest (Yuhuazhai) and opened on November 8, 2016. Line 4, which is basically parallel to Line 2 on its east except for the northern parts, runs from the North Square of the North Railway Station [Beikezhan (Beiguangchang)] to south (Hangtianxincheng) and was available publicly on December 26, 2018.[[90]](#cite_note-91) Line 5 opened on December 28, 2020. This line is 41.6 kilometers long, with 31 stations from Matengkong to Chuangxingang.[[91]](#cite_note-92)

Line 16 opened on June 27, 2023, and is 15.03 kilometers long, and runs from [Qinchuangyuanzhongxin](/w/index.php?title=Qinchuangyuanzhongxin_station&action=edit&redlink=1 "Qinchuangyuanzhongxin station (page does not exist)") to [Shijingli](/w/index.php?title=Shijingli_station&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shijingli station (page does not exist)") with 9 stations.[[92]](#cite_note-93) Four more lines are currently under construction, including an extension of Line 1.[[93]](#cite_note-94)

The subway system covers some of the most famous attractions, such as [Banpo Museum](/wiki/Banpo_Museum "Banpo Museum") (Banpo Station, Line 1), Bell and Drum Tower (Line 2), [Fortifications of Xi'an](/wiki/Fortifications_of_Xi%27an "Fortifications of Xi'an") (Line 2), the Giant Wild Goose Pagoda (Line 3 and Line 4), the Daminggong National Heritage Park (Line 4) and [Shaanxi History Museum](/wiki/Shaanxi_History_Museum "Shaanxi History Museum") (Line 2, 3 and 4), etc.[[94]](#cite_note-xian-95)

The first metro departure time for Line 1, 2, 3 and 4 is 6:00, the last metro departure time for Line 3 and 4 is 23:00, for Line 1 is 23:30, and for Line 2 is 23:50.[[95]](#cite_note-96)

On December 30, 2008, a fire accident occurred that was extinguished within an hour and all workers evacuated safely. Sixty-six hours later, on January 2, another fire occurred at another station on Line 2.[[96]](#cite_note-97)

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Rail")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/South_facade_of_Xi%27anbei_Railway_Station_20200429.jpg/250px-South_facade_of_Xi%27anbei_Railway_Station_20200429.jpg)](/wiki/File:South_facade_of_Xi%27anbei_Railway_Station_20200429.jpg)

[Xi'an North railway station](/wiki/Xi%27an_North_railway_station "Xi'an North railway station")

[Xi'an North railway station](/wiki/Xi%27an_North_railway_station "Xi'an North railway station"), situated a few miles to the north of the city centre, is the station for the high-speed trains of the [Zhengzhou–Xi'an High-Speed Railway](/wiki/Zhengzhou%E2%80%93Xi%27an_High-Speed_Railway "Zhengzhou–Xi'an High-Speed Railway"), part of the [Xuzhou–Lanzhou high-speed railway](/wiki/Xuzhou%E2%80%93Lanzhou_high-speed_railway "Xuzhou–Lanzhou high-speed railway"). Construction work on the Zhengzhou–Xi'an High-Speed Railway began on September 25, 2005, the railway opened for service on February 6, 2010.[[97]](#cite_note-98)[[98]](#cite_note-99) The railway made air service between Zhengzhou and Xi'an uncompetitive. All passenger flights between the two cities were suspended within 48 days of start of regular high-speed rail service.[[99]](#cite_note-100) With 34 platforms, Xi'an North was the largest railway station in [Northwest China](/wiki/Northwest_China "Northwest China") when it was opened.[[100]](#cite_note-gazette-101) Construction of the station began on September 19, 2008.[[101]](#cite_note-102) The station was opened on January 11, 2011.[[100]](#cite_note-gazette-101) It is also served by the [Datong–Xi'an high-speed railway](/wiki/Datong%E2%80%93Xi%27an_high-speed_railway "Datong–Xi'an high-speed railway"), which connects to [Beijing North](/wiki/Beijing_North_railway_station "Beijing North railway station"), the [Xi'an–Chengdu high-speed railway](/wiki/Xi%27an%E2%80%93Chengdu_high-speed_railway "Xi'an–Chengdu high-speed railway") and the [Yinchuan–Xi'an high-speed railway](/wiki/Yinchuan%E2%80%93Xi%27an_high-speed_railway "Yinchuan–Xi'an high-speed railway").

[Xi'an railway station](/wiki/Xi%27an_railway_station "Xi'an railway station") is located just north of Xi'an's walled city and is the main hub of conventional rail services in Shaanxi Province. It covers 597,000 square meters (6,430,000 [square feet](/wiki/Square_foot "Square foot")), has 6 passenger platforms and 11 passenger tracks. It provides 112 services to 80 000 people daily. Among the destinations served by direct trains from Xi'an are [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), [Zhengzhou](/wiki/Zhengzhou "Zhengzhou"), [Lanzhou](/wiki/Lanzhou "Lanzhou"), [Baoji](/wiki/Baoji "Baoji"), and [Mount Hua](/wiki/Mount_Hua "Mount Hua").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Airport%2C_Terminal_JP7562176.jpg/250px-Airport%2C_Terminal_JP7562176.jpg)](/wiki/File:Airport,_Terminal_JP7562176.jpg)

[Xi'an Xianyang International Airport](/wiki/Xi%27an_Xianyang_International_Airport "Xi'an Xianyang International Airport")

[Xi'an East](/wiki/Xi%27an_East_railway_station "Xi'an East railway station") and [Xi'an South](/wiki/Xi%27an_South_railway_station "Xi'an South railway station") are large stations now under construction to serve new high-speed lines being built, such as the [Xi'an–Chongqing HSR](/wiki/Xi%27an%E2%80%93Chongqing_high_speed_railway "Xi'an–Chongqing high speed railway") and the [Wuhan–Xi'an HSR](/wiki/Wuhan%E2%80%93Shiyan_high-speed_railway "Wuhan–Shiyan high-speed railway").

The city's other stations include [Xi'an West](/wiki/Xi%27an_West_railway_station "Xi'an West railway station"), [Yinzhen](/wiki/Yinzhen_railway_station "Yinzhen railway station"), Sanmincun, and Fangzhicheng.

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Air")]

[Xi'an Xianyang International Airport](/wiki/Xi%27an_Xianyang_International_Airport "Xi'an Xianyang International Airport") (airport code: XIY) is the major airport serving the city and it is the largest airport in the northwestern part of China. It is 25 miles northwest of Xi'an city center, and 8 miles northeast of the center of Xianyang.[[102]](#cite_note-yearbook-103) [China Eastern Airlines](/wiki/China_Eastern_Airlines "China Eastern Airlines"), [Hainan Airlines](/wiki/Hainan_Airlines "Hainan Airlines") and [China Southern Airlines](/wiki/China_Southern_Airlines "China Southern Airlines") are the main airlines using the airport. Terminal 3 and the second runway were opened on May 3, 2012.[[103]](#cite_note-104) Construction of Terminal 5 began in 2022 and was completed in September 2024.[[104]](#cite_note-105)

Other than linking to most Chinese cities, the airport also has flights to several major international destinations, including Tokyo, Seoul, London, Paris and Sydney.

### Resident artists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Resident artists")]

Xi'an is home to contemporary Chinese stars such as [Xu Wei](/wiki/Xu_Wei_(musician) "Xu Wei (musician)"),[[105]](#cite_note-106) [Zhang Chu](/wiki/Zhang_Chu_(singer) "Zhang Chu (singer)") and [Zheng Jun](/wiki/Zheng_Jun "Zheng Jun").

### Xi'an cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Xi'an cuisine")]

Main article: [Shaanxi cuisine](/wiki/Shaanxi_cuisine "Shaanxi cuisine")

*[Yangrou paomo](/wiki/Paomo "Paomo")* (flat bread soaked in lamb soup; simplified Chinese: 羊肉泡馍; traditional Chinese: 羊肉泡饃; pinyin: *Yángròu pàomó*)[[106]](#cite_note-107) is a well known Xi'anese dish.

*[Liang pi](/wiki/Liangpi "Liangpi")* (cold rice noodles; simplified Chinese: 凉皮; traditional Chinese: 涼皮; pinyin: *liángpí*) are wheat or rice noodles served cold with vinegar and chili oil.

*[Biangbiang mian](/wiki/Biangbiang_noodles "Biangbiang noodles")*, also known as *youpo chemian* (simplified Chinese: 油泼扯面; traditional Chinese: 油潑扯麵; pinyin: *Yóupō chěmiàn*), are thick and long hand-pulled noodles, typically served with red hot pepper.

*[Roujiamo](/wiki/Roujiamo "Roujiamo")* (meat buns; simplified Chinese: 肉夾馍; traditional Chinese: 肉夾饃; pinyin: *Ròujiāmó*) is a bun filled with pork.

### Qinqiang

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Qinqiang")]

[Qinqiang](/wiki/Qinqiang "Qinqiang") (Voice of Qin) is the oldest and most extensive of the four major types of Chinese opera.[[107]](#cite_note-108)[[108]](#cite_note-109)[[109]](#cite_note-110) Another one would be the Empress of the Great Tang is China's first Tang dynasty dance and music show. The story is based on the life of the famous Chinese historical figure [Empress Wu Zetian](/wiki/Wu_Zetian "Wu Zetian") of the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty"). Through live performances by a classical Chinese orchestra and state-of-the-art stage design, this show will take you back to the glory of the legendary Empress Wu Zetian and the Great Tang Empire.

### Chinese traditional religion and Taoism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Chinese traditional religion and Taoism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/The_Temple_of_the_Town_Deity_in_Xi%27an_13_2013-09.jpg/250px-The_Temple_of_the_Town_Deity_in_Xi%27an_13_2013-09.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Temple_of_the_Town_Deity_in_Xi%27an_13_2013-09.jpg)

A pavilion of the City God Temple of Xi'an

The most influential religions in Xi'an are the [Chinese traditional religion](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion "Chinese folk religion") and [Taoist](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism") [schools](/wiki/Taoist_schools "Taoist schools"), represented by many major and minor temples. Among these there are a [City God Temple](/wiki/City_God_(China) "City God (China)"), completely reconstructed in the 2010s, and a [Temple of Confucius](/wiki/Temple_of_Confucius "Temple of Confucius").

### Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Christianity")]

See also: [Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Xi'an](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Xi%27an "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Xi'an")

The first recorded Christian missionary in China was [Alopen](/wiki/Alopen "Alopen"), a [Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_language "Syriac language")-speaker, who arrived in Xi'an (then known as Chang'an) in 635 along the [Silk Road](/wiki/Silk_Road "Silk Road"). The [Nestorian Stele](/wiki/Nestorian_Stele "Nestorian Stele"), now located in Xi'an's [Beilin Museum](/wiki/Stele_Forest "Stele Forest"), is a [Tang Chinese](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") [stele](/wiki/Stele "Stele") erected in 781 that documents the 150 years of early [Christianity in China](/wiki/Christianity_in_China "Christianity in China") following Alopen.[[34]](#cite_note-hill-108-35) It is a 279-centimeter-tall (110-[inch](/wiki/Inch "Inch")) [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") block with text in both [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") and [Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_language "Syriac language") describing the existence of Christian communities in several cities in northern China. The [Daqin Pagoda](/wiki/Daqin_Pagoda "Daqin Pagoda"), a Buddhist pagoda in [Zhouzhi County](/wiki/Zhouzhi_County "Zhouzhi County") of Xi'an, has been suggested to have originally been a [Nestorian](/wiki/Nestorian "Nestorian") Christian church from the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty").[[110]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-111)

Baptist missionaries from England ran a hospital in Xi'an.[[111]](#cite_note-112) In 1892, [Arthur Gostick Shorrock](/wiki/Arthur_Gostick_Shorrock "Arthur Gostick Shorrock")[[112]](#cite_note-Shorrock1926-113) and Moir Duncan[[113]](#cite_note-DuncanPapers-114) founded the *Sianfu Mission*, in present-day Xi'an.[[114]](#cite_note-Burt1925-115)[[115]](#cite_note-Glover1914-116)[[116]](#cite_note-Duncan1900-117)

### Islam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Islam")]

Xi'an has a minority Muslim community, most of these Muslims are from the [Hui](/wiki/Hui_people "Hui people") group, there are an estimated 50,000 Hui Muslims in Xi'an.[[117]](#cite_note-118) There are seven mosques in Xi'an, the best known being the [Great Mosque](/wiki/Great_Mosque_of_Xi%27an "Great Mosque of Xi'an").[[118]](#cite_note-119) The Great Mosque, located in the city center, is one of the oldest and most significant mosques in China. It was built during the Tang Dynasty and has been renovated and expanded over the centuries. The mosque reflects a unique blend of Islamic and traditional Chinese architectural styles.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Public archaeology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Public archaeology")]

The [Shaanxi Provincial Cultural Heritage Administration](/w/index.php?title=Shaanxi_Provincial_Cultural_Heritage_Administration&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shaanxi Provincial Cultural Heritage Administration (page does not exist)") has initiated "Archaeology Live" programs at several active excavation sites within the city, such as the [Hanjing Site](/w/index.php?title=Hanjing_Site&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hanjing Site (page does not exist)"). These programs allow the public to observe archaeological work in progress and learn about conservation methods, representing a significant step in public outreach for Chinese archaeology.[[119]](#cite_note-120)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/%E9%95%BF%E5%AE%89_%E6%B2%A3%E4%B8%9C%E5%A4%A7%E9%81%93%E6%B2%A3%E6%B2%B3%E6%A1%A5%E4%B8%8A%E4%B8%9C%E6%9C%9B%E5%B8%82%E5%8C%BA.jpg/250px-%E9%95%BF%E5%AE%89_%E6%B2%A3%E4%B8%9C%E5%A4%A7%E9%81%93%E6%B2%A3%E6%B2%B3%E6%A1%A5%E4%B8%8A%E4%B8%9C%E6%9C%9B%E5%B8%82%E5%8C%BA.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E9%95%BF%E5%AE%89_%E6%B2%A3%E4%B8%9C%E5%A4%A7%E9%81%93%E6%B2%A3%E6%B2%B3%E6%A1%A5%E4%B8%8A%E4%B8%9C%E6%9C%9B%E5%B8%82%E5%8C%BA.jpg)

Fenghe Bridge

As part of the [China Western Development](/wiki/China_Western_Development "China Western Development") policy, Xi'an became a major target for accelerated attention, particularly in information technology, advanced materials, aerospace, energy, and chemical engineering.[[60]](#cite_note-:9-61): 186 From 1997 to 2006, the industrial output value of Xi'an's service industry increased at an annual average rate of 13 percent, compared to traditional service industries of 0.74 percent, representing a growth from US$8.113 billion to US$25.85 billion.[[120]](#cite_note-chinacenter-121)

In 2020, Xi'an was ranked as a Beta- (global second tier) city by the [Globalization and World Cities Research Network](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network "Globalization and World Cities Research Network").[[12]](#cite_note-:1-13) Xi'an was ranked in 2020 as one of the world's top 100 financial centers, according to the [Global Financial Centres Index](/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index").[[121]](#cite_note-GFCI-122)

Important industries include equipment manufacturing, tourism, and service outsourcing.[[122]](#cite_note-official_website_for_Shaanxi-123)[*[non-primary source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research#Primary,_secondary_and_tertiary_sources "Wikipedia:No original research")*] The manufacturing industry had an annual output of RMB 36.5 billion, accounting for 44.5 percent of the city's total.[[123]](#cite_note-city_info-124) Furthermore, as one of China's four ancient capitals,[[124]](#cite_note-125) Xi'an's many cultural sites, including the [Terracotta Army](/wiki/Terracotta_Army "Terracotta Army"), the [City Wall of Xi'an](/wiki/City_Wall_of_Xi%27an "City Wall of Xi'an"), and the [Famen Temple](/wiki/Famen_Temple "Famen Temple"), make tourism an important industry as well. In 2019, tourists visiting Xi'an exceeded 300 million, earning a total income of RMB 314.6 billion.[[125]](#cite_note-126) On average, revenue increases by 36.4 percent per year, and foreign-exchange earnings (530 million in 2009) increase by around 35.8 percent.[[123]](#cite_note-city_info-124)

Xi'an is also one of the first service outsourcing cities in China, with over 800 corporations in the industry. The city's output value from this sector exceeded RMB 23 billion in 2008. Employment in the sector doubled from 1997 to 2006, from a base of 60,000, and computer consulting also doubled from 16,000 to 32,000.[[120]](#cite_note-chinacenter-121) As a result of the importance of the software-outsourcing industry, the city planned construction of a Software New Town, which is scheduled to be completed in 2015 with 30 billion RMB investment.[[123]](#cite_note-city_info-124) Other major export goods include lighting equipment and automobile parts, while its major import goods are mechanical and electrical products. Internationally, Xi'an's largest trade partner is the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[123]](#cite_note-city_info-124)

The [Xi'an International Trade and Logistics Park](/wiki/Xi%27an_International_Trade_%26_Logistics_Park "Xi'an International Trade & Logistics Park") covers about thirty-five square miles and was conceived of as model for logistics-based [urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization_in_China "Urbanization in China").[[126]](#cite_note-:Curtis&Klaus-127): 120 The park is a major [dry port](/wiki/Dry_port "Dry port") and rail container center.[[126]](#cite_note-:Curtis&Klaus-127): 120 More China-Europe Freight Train (CEFT) rails depart from or return to Xi'an than any other city.[[126]](#cite_note-:Curtis&Klaus-127): 120–121

### Software and technological industries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Software and technological industries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/%E9%9B%81%E5%A1%94_%E5%9C%A8%E9%94%A6%E4%B8%9A%E8%B7%AF%E4%B8%8A%E7%9C%8B%E9%AB%98%E6%96%B0%E5%95%86%E5%8A%A1%E5%8C%BA_01.jpg/250px-%E9%9B%81%E5%A1%94_%E5%9C%A8%E9%94%A6%E4%B8%9A%E8%B7%AF%E4%B8%8A%E7%9C%8B%E9%AB%98%E6%96%B0%E5%95%86%E5%8A%A1%E5%8C%BA_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E9%9B%81%E5%A1%94_%E5%9C%A8%E9%94%A6%E4%B8%9A%E8%B7%AF%E4%B8%8A%E7%9C%8B%E9%AB%98%E6%96%B0%E5%95%86%E5%8A%A1%E5%8C%BA_01.jpg)

Xi'an Hi-Tech Industries Development Zone

The major industrial zone in Xi'an is the [Xi'an Economic and Technological Development Zone](/wiki/Xi%27an_Economic_and_Technological_Development_Zone "Xi'an Economic and Technological Development Zone"). The [Jiangcungou landfill](/wiki/Jiangcungou_landfill "Jiangcungou landfill") in Xi'an was China's largest landfill site before its closure in 2019.[[127]](#cite_note-128)

The growing economy of the city also supports the development of a software industry, and the city is a pioneer in software industry in China. The Xi'an Software Park within the Xi'an Hi-Tech Industries Development Zone (XDZ) has attracted over 1,085 corporations and 106,000 employees as of 2012.[[128]](#cite_note-129) Xi'an is described as having" large pool of cheap human resources from the 100 universities in the area, it hoovers up around 3,000 computer graduates every year, each earning approximately $120 a month—half the wages for the equivalent job in Beijing."[[129]](#cite_note-130)[[130]](#cite_note-131)

### Aerospace industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Aerospace industry")]

In November 2006, Xi'an and the China Aerospace Science and Technology Corporation jointly set up Xi'an Aerospace Science and Technology Industrial Base. From its establishment, the base has focused on the development of the civil space industry, including equipment manufacturing, [software](/wiki/Software "Software") and service [outsourcing](/wiki/Outsourcing "Outsourcing"), new materials and solar [photovoltaics](/wiki/Photovoltaics "Photovoltaics").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Apart from the core area, the base will cover Xi'an and the [Guanzhong](/wiki/Guanzhong "Guanzhong") area and the expansion zone will reach other parts of [Northwest China](/wiki/Northwest_China "Northwest China") and [Southwest China](/wiki/Southwest_China "Southwest China"). It is expected that by 2012 the total industry output can reach 2.8 billion us dollars with about 10 to 20 brand products with intellectual property rights and 5 to 8 products with global competitiveness.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2008, after the launch of the initial aerospace center in Shanghai, the PRC is constructing another civil aerospace center in the Shaanxi province. The State Development and Reform Commission approved the planning of Xi'an National Civil Aerospace Industrial Base on December 26, 2007. The National Civil Aerospace Industrial Base of Xi'an, set to cover 23 km2 (8.9 sq mi), will focus on developing satellites, new materials, energies, IT and other technologies for civil applications.[[131]](#cite_note-132)

## Education and research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Education and research")]

Further information: [List of universities in China](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_China "List of universities in China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/XJTU_campus.jpg/500px-XJTU_campus.jpg)](/wiki/File:XJTU_campus.jpg)

Xi'an Jiaotong University

Xi'an has many [highly ranked educational institutions](/wiki/Rankings_of_universities_in_China "Rankings of universities in China"), with the seven universities listed in 147 [National Key Universities](/wiki/National_Key_Universities "National Key Universities") under the [Double First-Class Construction](/wiki/Double_First-Class_Construction "Double First-Class Construction") ranking fourth nationwide (tie) with [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou"), [Wuhan](/wiki/Wuhan "Wuhan") and [Chengdu](/wiki/Chengdu "Chengdu") (only after [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai") and [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing")). There are a total of 60 universities in the city,[[132]](#cite_note-133) with the two famous ones being the [Xi'an Jiaotong University](/wiki/Xi%27an_Jiaotong_University "Xi'an Jiaotong University") (西安交通大学) and the [Northwestern Polytechnical University](/wiki/Northwestern_Polytechnical_University "Northwestern Polytechnical University") (西北工业大学), which were ranked 92 and 101-150 worldwide, respectively.[[133]](#cite_note-134) Other ones also include [Xidian University](/wiki/Xidian_University "Xidian University") (西安电子科技大学), [Chang'an University](/wiki/Chang%27an_University "Chang'an University") (长安大学), [Northwest University](/wiki/Northwest_University,_China "Northwest University, China") (西北大学), [Northwest University of Political Science and Law](/wiki/Northwest_University_of_Political_Science_and_Law "Northwest University of Political Science and Law") (西北政法大学) and; [Shaanxi Normal University](/wiki/Shaanxi_Normal_University "Shaanxi Normal University") (陕西师范大学).[[16]](#cite_note-:2-17)

The city was ranked 124th globally by the QS Best Student Cities Rankings in 2025.[[134]](#cite_note-135)

Xi'an is a world leading city for scientific research and as of 2023, it ranked among the world's top 20 cities and China's top 10 cities by scientific research output by the [Nature Index](/wiki/Nature_Index "Nature Index").[[135]](#cite_note-:3-136) It ranked first in [West China](/wiki/Western_China "Western China") region, with a combined population of almost 300 million. The city also hosted the 2011 World Horticultural Exposition.[[136]](#cite_note-137)

### Sites

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xi%27an&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Sites")]

Because of the city's many historical monuments and a plethora of ancient ruins and tombs in the vicinity,[[10]](#cite_note-br-11) tourism has been an important component of the local economy, and the Xi'an region is one of the most popular tourist destinations in China.[[10]](#cite_note-br-11)

The city has many important historical sites, and some are ongoing archaeological projects, such as the [Mausoleum of Qin Shi Huang](/wiki/Mausoleum_of_the_First_Qin_Emperor "Mausoleum of the First Qin Emperor") and his [Terracotta Army](/wiki/Terracotta_Army "Terracotta Army"). There are several burial mounds, tombs of the [Zhou dynasty](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty") kings located in the city.[[8]](#cite_note-encarta-9) Xi'an also contains some 800 royal [mausoleums](/wiki/Mausoleum "Mausoleum") and tombs from the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty"),[[137]](#cite_note-columbia-138) with some of them yielding hundreds of sculpted clay soldiers, and remains of sacrificial temples from the Han era.[[137]](#cite_note-columbia-138) The city has numerous [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") pagodas and is noted for its history museum and its stele forest, which is housed in an 11th-century Confucian temple containing large stone tablets from various dynasties.[[137]](#cite_note-columbia-138)

Some of the most well-known sites in Xi'an are:

* The city is surrounded by a well-preserved [city wall](/wiki/City_wall_of_Xi%27an "City wall of Xi'an") which was reconstructed in the 14th century during the early [Ming dynasty](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty") and was based on the inner imperial palace of [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty").
* The Mausoleum of [Qin Shi Huang](/wiki/Qin_Shi_Huang "Qin Shi Huang") and his [Terracotta Army](/wiki/Terracotta_Army "Terracotta Army") are located 40 km (25 mi) to the east of the city center, in the city's suburbs.
* The [Bell Tower](/wiki/Bell_Tower_of_Xi%27an "Bell Tower of Xi'an") and [Drum Tower](/wiki/Drum_Tower_of_Xi%27an "Drum Tower of Xi'an"), both are located at the city's central axis
* The city's [Muslim Quarter](/wiki/Xi%27an_Muslim_Quarter "Xi'an Muslim Quarter"), which is home to the [Great Mosque of Xi'an](/wiki/Great_Mosque_of_Xi%27an "Great Mosque of Xi'an")
* The [Giant Wild Goose Pagoda](/wiki/Giant_Wild_Goose_Pagoda "Giant Wild Goose Pagoda") and [Small Wild Goose Pagoda](/wiki/Small_Wild_Goose_Pagoda "Small Wild Goose Pagoda") are both spectacular towers and both are well over 1,000 years old and have survived great earthquakes.
* The [Stele Forest](/wiki/Stele_Forest "Stele Forest") is famous for its numerous historic inscriptions and stoneworks.[[138]](#cite_note-139)
* The [Famen Temple](/wiki/Famen_Temple "Famen Temple") and its towering pagoda located 120 kilometers (75 miles) west of Xi'an.
* [Xi Ming Temple](/wiki/Xi_Ming_Temple "Xi Ming Temple"), a historic Buddhist temple
* [Wolong Temple](/wiki/Wolong_Temple "Wolong Temple"), a Buddhist temple at Kaitong lane near the [Stele Forest](/wiki/Stele_Forest "Stele Forest")
* [Xingjiao Temple](/wiki/Xingjiao_Temple "Xingjiao Temple") at Shaolin Yuan
* [Jianfu Temple](/wiki/Jianfu_Temple "Jianfu Temple"), which is associated with the [Small Wild Goose Pagoda](/wiki/Small_Wild_Goose_Pagoda "Small Wild Goose Pagoda")
* The [Banpo](/wiki/Banpo "Banpo") Neolithic village
* The [Shaanxi History Museum](/wiki/Shaanxi_History_Museum "Shaanxi History Museum"), which has a large collection of historical artifacts
* The [Xi'an Museum](/wiki/Xi%27an_Museum "Xi'an Museum"), located near the [Small Wild Goose Pagoda](/wiki/Small_Wild_Goose_Pagoda "Small Wild Goose Pagoda")
* [Huaqing Hot Springs](/wiki/Huaqing_Pool "Huaqing Pool"), at the foot of Mt. Li, have a history of 6,000 years, the adjacent Huaqing Palace has a history of 3,000 years. Ranked among the Hundred Famous Gardens in China, it also has the status as a [National Cultural Relic Protection Unit and a National Key Scenic Area](/wiki/Major_Site_to_Be_Protected_for_Its_Historical_and_Cultural_Value_at_the_National_Level "Major Site to Be Protected for Its Historical and Cultural Value at the National Level").
* [Daming Palace National Heritage Park](/wiki/Daming_Palace_National_Heritage_Park "Daming Palace National Heritage Park"), site of the former royal residence of the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") emperors
* [Mount Li](/wiki/Mount_Li "Mount Li") National Forest Park
* [Mount Zhongnan](/wiki/Mount_Zhongnan "Mount Zhongnan") National Forest Park
* Datang Everbright City
